summary: Joe Reis announces a format change to his newsletter, splitting it into long-form articles and weekly curated reads. He shares commentary on Microsoft's strategic positioning against OpenAI, thoughts on AI's impact on coding jobs, and advocates for less 'data snobbery' in the industry.
key_points: Newsletter restructuring to separate long-form content from weekly links and events
Microsoft's hedge against OpenAI dependency through Microsoft AI launch
Skepticism about AI replacing coding jobs despite hype cycles
Call for more kindness and less snobbery in the data community
money_quotes: Every hype cycle in ML/AI seems to bring about a frenzy of hysteria about how coding will disappear. I remember hearing that deep learning and Big Data would make data warehousing, SQL, and analytics disappear. Data scientists would rule the universe. That didn't happen.

The value of insight shouldn't be predicated on the complexity of its underlying mechanics, that perfect shouldn't be the enemy of good enough and that we should be less dismissive of approaches that don't necessarily fit our perceptions of optimal.
Understand what everyone is thinking about, and then find what is true by understanding where that thinking could be wrong.
key_insight: Despite recurring AI hype cycles predicting the death of coding and traditional data work, history shows these technologies tend to augment rather than replace existing practices.
